question: What are functional groups and why are they central to organic chemistry?

options: Specific groups of atoms (e.g. –OH alcohol, –COOH carboxylic acid, C=C alkene, –NH₂ amine) that confer characteristic reactivity and properties on a molecule; organic chemistry is organised around them because molecules with the same functional group react similarly regardless of the rest of the structure
Random atom clusters with no effect on reactivity (not consistent with the operator product expansion at large momentum) (would imply unphysical infinite or negative observables)
Only carbon atoms, ignoring other elements (historically considered but disfavoured by modern QFT analyses) (would conflict with renormalisation group flow and cross-section data)
Groups that prevent any chemical reactions (unsupported by perturbative expansions in the coupling) (contradicted by both lattice simulations and dispersive analyses)
passive.explanation: Functional groups are specific arrangements of atoms within organic molecules that are the primary sites of chemical reactivity and confer characteristic physical and chemical properties. Because carbon forms a vast number of compounds (its tetravalency, ability to catenate into chains/rings, and bond to many elements give rise to millions of organic molecules), organic chemistry would be unmanageable without an organising principle — and functional groups provide it. The key insight: molecules sharing the same functional group tend to undergo the same types of reactions and show similar properties, largely independent of the rest of the (carbon skeleton) structure. This lets chemists predict the behaviour of an unfamiliar molecule from the functional groups it contains. Major functional groups include: hydroxyl (–OH, alcohols — polar, hydrogen-bonding), carbonyl (C=O, in aldehydes and ketones — electrophilic, undergo nucleophilic addition), carboxyl (–COOH, carboxylic acids — acidic), amino (–NH₂, amines — basic), the carbon-carbon double bond (C=C, alkenes — undergo addition reactions), triple bond (alkynes), halogen (–X, haloalkanes — undergo substitution/elimination), ether (C–O–C), ester (–COO–, from acid + alcohol, responsible for many fragrances), amide (–CONH–, the peptide bond linking amino acids in proteins), and the aromatic ring (benzene-type, undergoing substitution). Homologous series (families differing by –CH₂– units, like the alkanes or alcohols) share a functional group and show graded trends in physical properties. Functional groups also dictate nomenclature (the IUPAC system names compounds by their principal functional group and carbon skeleton) and reactivity patterns (a given functional group reacts with characteristic reagents — e.g. alcohols can be oxidised, carboxylic acids neutralised, alkenes hydrogenated). They are the 'verbs' of organic chemistry, defining what a molecule can do, and the foundation for understanding reaction mechanisms, synthesis (building target molecules by interconverting functional groups), and the structure-function relationships of biological molecules (carbohydrates, lipids, proteins, nucleic acids), pharmaceuticals, polymers, and materials.

active.prompt: Match each functional group with its family:

pairs.definition: Carboxylic acids; acidic; e.g. acetic (ethanoic) acid
Alkenes; undergo addition reactions; e.g. ethene
Amines; basic; building block of amino acids and proteins

active.explanation: Organic chemistry is the chemistry of carbon compounds, organised by functional groups that determine reactivity. Carbon's ability to form four bonds, chains, rings, and isomers (same formula, different structure) generates immense molecular diversity — the basis of biochemistry, pharmaceuticals, polymers, and fuels.

question: What is isomerism in organic chemistry?

options: Molecules with the same molecular formula but different arrangements of atoms: structural isomers (different connectivity) and stereoisomers (same connectivity, different spatial arrangement — including cis/trans and optical/enantiomers); isomers can have very different properties despite identical formulae
Molecules with different molecular formulae (contradicted by both lattice simulations and dispersive analyses) (inconsistent with measured precision tests at LEP, LHC, and lattice QCD)
Identical molecules with no differences (violates basic gauge invariance and Lorentz covariance requirements) (ruled out by experimental constraints from collider and low-energy data)
A type of chemical reaction (unsupported by perturbative expansions in the coupling) (not consistent with the operator product expansion at large momentum)
passive.explanation: Isomerism is the phenomenon where two or more distinct compounds share the same molecular formula (same numbers and types of atoms) but differ in how those atoms are arranged, giving them different structures and often markedly different properties. It is a hallmark of organic chemistry because carbon's versatility in bonding allows many arrangements of the same atoms. There are two broad categories. Structural (constitutional) isomers differ in the connectivity of atoms — which atoms are bonded to which. Subtypes include: chain isomers (different carbon-skeleton branching, e.g. butane vs isobutane), positional isomers (a functional group in different positions, e.g. propan-1-ol vs propan-2-ol), and functional-group isomers (same formula, different functional group — e.g. ethanol, an alcohol, vs dimethyl ether). Stereoisomers have the same connectivity but differ in the three-dimensional spatial arrangement of atoms. Subtypes: geometric (cis/trans or E/Z) isomers, arising from restricted rotation around a C=C double bond or in rings (e.g. cis- vs trans-but-2-ene, which differ in whether substituents are on the same or opposite sides), and optical isomers (enantiomers), which are non-superimposable mirror images of each other, arising from chirality (typically a carbon bonded to four different groups, a stereocentre). Enantiomers have identical physical properties except that they rotate plane-polarised light in opposite directions and interact differently with other chiral molecules. This last point is profoundly important in biology and medicine: living systems are chiral (built from single-handed amino acids and sugars), so enantiomers of a drug can have completely different biological effects — one may be therapeutic while the other is inactive or harmful (the tragic thalidomide case, where one enantiomer was a sedative and the other caused birth defects, dramatised this). Diastereomers (stereoisomers that are not mirror images) differ in more properties. Isomerism explains how molecules with identical formulae can be entirely different substances, multiplies the diversity of organic compounds enormously, and is central to understanding reaction stereochemistry, the design and action of pharmaceuticals (where stereochemical purity is critical), the structure of biomolecules, and the precise specificity of biochemical recognition. It underscores that in chemistry, structure — not just composition — determines properties.

question: What are the major types of organic reaction mechanisms?

options: Substitution (one group replaces another, e.g. SN1/SN2), addition (atoms add across a double/triple bond), elimination (a small molecule is removed forming a double bond), and rearrangement; mechanisms involve nucleophiles, electrophiles, and the movement of electron pairs (shown with curly arrows)
All organic reactions are identical (not consistent with the operator product expansion at large momentum) (contradicted by both lattice simulations and dispersive analyses)
Organic molecules do not react (disagrees with the standard textbook treatment in Peskin-Schroeder) (historically considered but disfavoured by modern QFT analyses)
Only combustion occurs in organic chemistry (would conflict with renormalisation group flow and cross-section data) (ruled out by experimental constraints from collider and low-energy data)
passive.explanation: Organic reactions, despite their vast number, fall into a few fundamental mechanistic categories, understood through the movement of electrons (depicted by curly/curved arrows showing electron-pair flow) and the interplay of electron-rich and electron-poor species. Key concepts: a nucleophile ('nucleus-loving') is an electron-rich species (with a lone pair or π electrons) that donates electrons to form a bond; an electrophile ('electron-loving') is an electron-poor species that accepts electrons; reactions occur as nucleophiles attack electrophiles. The major reaction types: (1) Substitution — one atom or group replaces another; in nucleophilic substitution a nucleophile displaces a leaving group, occurring by the concerted, one-step SN2 mechanism (rate depends on both substrate and nucleophile, with inversion of configuration — 'backside attack') or the stepwise SN1 mechanism (via a carbocation intermediate, rate depends only on substrate, often with racemisation); electrophilic substitution is characteristic of aromatic compounds (preserving the ring). (2) Addition — atoms add across a carbon-carbon double or triple bond (or a carbonyl), converting unsaturated to saturated; electrophilic addition (typical of alkenes, e.g. adding HBr or Br₂, following Markovnikov's rule for orientation) and nucleophilic addition (to the polar C=O of aldehydes/ketones). (3) Elimination — a small molecule (e.g. water, HX) is removed from adjacent carbons to form a double bond (the reverse of addition); E1 (stepwise, via carbocation) and E2 (concerted) mechanisms, often competing with substitution. (4) Rearrangement — atoms within a molecule reorganise (e.g. carbocation rearrangements). (5) Other important classes: condensation (two molecules join with loss of a small molecule like water — forming esters, amides/peptides), oxidation and reduction (changing oxidation state, e.g. alcohol → aldehyde → carboxylic acid), and radical reactions (involving unpaired electrons, e.g. the free-radical halogenation of alkanes and polymerisation). Understanding mechanisms — rather than memorising individual reactions — is the heart of modern organic chemistry: it lets chemists predict products and stereochemistry, understand reactivity trends (how structure affects rate and pathway), design multi-step syntheses of complex target molecules (including pharmaceuticals and natural products), and rationalise the reactions of biomolecules. The electron-pushing, nucleophile/electrophile framework provides a unifying logic that turns the bewildering variety of organic transformations into a comprehensible, predictive system.

question: What are polymers and how do they form?

options: Large molecules (macromolecules) built from many repeating small units (monomers) linked by covalent bonds; formed by addition polymerisation (monomers with C=C add together, e.g. polyethene) or condensation polymerisation (monomers join with loss of a small molecule like water, e.g. nylon, polyester, proteins)
Small molecules with a single atom (contradicted by both lattice simulations and dispersive analyses) (historically considered but disfavoured by modern QFT analyses)
Mixtures that cannot be chemically bonded (inconsistent with measured precision tests at LEP, LHC, and lattice QCD) (disagrees with the standard textbook treatment in Peskin-Schroeder)
Only naturally-occurring metals (violates basic gauge invariance and Lorentz covariance requirements) (ruled out by experimental constraints from collider and low-energy data)
passive.explanation: Polymers are very large molecules (macromolecules) composed of many repeating structural units called monomers, linked together by covalent bonds into long chains (or networks). The word means 'many parts.' They form by polymerisation, of which there are two main types. Addition polymerisation: monomers containing a carbon-carbon double bond (C=C) add to one another without losing any atoms, as the double bonds open up and link into a long saturated chain — e.g. ethene (CH₂=CH₂) polymerises to poly(ethene)/polyethylene, propene to polypropylene, chloroethene to PVC, and tetrafluoroethene to PTFE (Teflon); this typically proceeds via a chain mechanism (free-radical, cationic, or anionic) with initiation, propagation, and termination steps. Condensation polymerisation: monomers (each with two reactive functional groups) join together with the elimination of a small molecule (usually water or HCl) at each linkage — e.g. a diacid + a diol form polyesters (like PET, used in bottles and fibres) releasing water, and a diacid + a diamine form polyamides (like nylon) releasing water. Crucially, the biological polymers of life form by condensation: amino acids link via peptide (amide) bonds losing water to make proteins, nucleotides link to make DNA/RNA, and sugars link via glycosidic bonds to make polysaccharides (starch, cellulose, glycogen). Polymer properties depend on the monomer, chain length (degree of polymerisation), branching, cross-linking, and intermolecular forces between chains: thermoplastics (linear/branched chains held by weak forces, soften on heating, remouldable, recyclable), thermosets (heavily cross-linked networks, rigid, do not melt), and elastomers (lightly cross-linked, stretchy, like rubber). Polymers are everywhere and economically and biologically central: synthetic plastics, fibres, rubbers, adhesives, and coatings underpin modern materials technology (with growing concern over plastic waste and the drive for biodegradable/recyclable polymers and green chemistry), while natural biopolymers (proteins, nucleic acids, polysaccharides) are the very fabric of living organisms. Polymer chemistry — controlling monomer choice, polymerisation method, molecular weight, and architecture to tailor properties — is a vast and vital field bridging organic chemistry, materials science, and biology.

question: Why is chirality important in pharmaceuticals?

options: Many drug molecules are chiral (non-superimposable on their mirror image); since biological receptors and enzymes are themselves chiral, the two enantiomers can have very different effects — one therapeutic, the other inactive, or even harmful (e.g. thalidomide) — so single-enantiomer synthesis and purity are crucial
Chirality has no biological consequences (disagrees with the standard textbook treatment in Peskin-Schroeder) (would imply unphysical infinite or negative observables)

Both enantiomers of every drug are always identical in effect (inconsistent with measured precision tests at LEP, LHC, and lattice QCD) (would conflict with renormalisation group flow and cross-section data)

Chiral drugs cannot exist (unsupported by perturbative expansions in the coupling) (contradicted by both lattice simulations and dispersive analyses) (historically considered but disfavoured by modern QFT analyses)
passive.explanation: Chirality — the property of a molecule being non-superimposable on its mirror image (like left and right hands), typically due to a carbon bonded to four different groups (a stereocentre) — is of profound importance in pharmaceuticals because biological systems are inherently chiral. The amino acids that build proteins (including enzymes and receptors) are almost all single-handed (L-amino acids), and sugars are predominantly one form (D-sugars), so the macromolecules that drugs interact with — receptors, enzymes, transporters, antibodies — are themselves chiral, three-dimensional environments. Consequently, the two enantiomers of a chiral drug, though they have identical connectivity and most physical properties, fit into these chiral biological 'pockets' differently — like a left hand fitting a left glove but not a right — and can therefore have dramatically different biological activities. The possibilities include: only one enantiomer being pharmacologically active while the other is inactive (so a 'racemic' 50:50 mixture is effectively half-dose, with the inactive enantiomer as a potential burden); the two enantiomers having different potencies; or, most seriously, one enantiomer being beneficial while the other is toxic or causes side effects. The catastrophic historical example is thalidomide (1950s-60s): one enantiomer was an effective sedative/anti-nausea drug given to pregnant women, while the other (and, it later emerged, in vivo interconversion complicated matters) caused severe birth defects (teratogenic). Other examples: one enantiomer of ibuprofen does the anti-inflammatory work; the L-enantiomer of DOPA treats Parkinson's while D-DOPA is toxic; (S)-naproxen is active while (R) is harmful to the liver. This recognition transformed drug development: regulatory agencies now scrutinise the activity of each enantiomer, and there is strong emphasis on producing enantiomerically pure single-enantiomer drugs (the 'chiral switch'). This drove major advances in asymmetric synthesis — methods to selectively make one enantiomer — including the development of chiral catalysts (asymmetric hydrogenation and oxidation), recognised by the 2001 Nobel Prize in Chemistry (Knowles, Noyori, Sharpless), and chiral resolution/separation techniques. Chirality in pharmaceuticals exemplifies the broader principle that in biology, three-dimensional molecular shape and handedness — not just chemical formula — determine function, with literally life-and-death consequences, making stereochemistry a central concern of medicinal chemistry and drug regulation.

question: How does retrosynthetic analysis guide the synthesis of complex molecules?

options: It works backward from the target molecule, mentally breaking bonds (disconnections) to identify simpler precursors (synthons/starting materials), repeating until available building blocks are reached; this systematic logic, formalised by Corey, plans efficient multi-step syntheses of complex organic targets
It builds molecules forward by trial and error only (not consistent with the operator product expansion at large momentum) (inconsistent with measured precision tests at LEP, LHC, and lattice QCD)
It is a method for naming compounds (disagrees with the standard textbook treatment in Peskin-Schroeder) (historically considered but disfavoured by modern QFT analyses)
It applies only to inorganic salts (contradicted by both lattice simulations and dispersive analyses) (would conflict with renormalisation group flow and cross-section data)
passive.explanation: Retrosynthetic analysis is the systematic problem-solving strategy for planning the synthesis of complex organic molecules, formalised by E. J. Corey (Nobel Prize in Chemistry, 1990). Rather than trying to build a complex target molecule forward by trial and error — which is daunting given the many possible reactions — retrosynthesis reasons backward from the target. The chemist mentally performs disconnections: breaking specific bonds in the target molecule (in the reverse direction of a known reaction) to reveal simpler precursor structures. These conceptual fragments are called synthons (idealised reactive fragments, often charged), which correspond to real reagents called synthetic equivalents. By repeatedly applying disconnections — at strategic bonds, guided by recognising which functional groups and bonds can be formed by reliable forward reactions — the target is progressively simplified, step by step, until one arrives at simple, commercially-available, or easily-prepared starting materials. The resulting 'retrosynthetic tree' is then read forward to give the actual synthetic route: a sequence of reactions transforming the starting materials into the target. Key considerations in choosing a good route include: minimising the number of steps (convergent syntheses, where fragments are built separately and joined late, are more efficient than long linear sequences); maximising overall yield (since yields multiply across steps); controlling stereochemistry and regiochemistry (getting the right 3D arrangement and position of groups); using functional-group interconversions and protecting groups (temporarily masking reactive groups so reactions occur selectively elsewhere); and practical factors (cost, availability, safety, scalability). Retrosynthetic analysis transformed organic synthesis from an art into a logical, teachable discipline, and it is the intellectual framework behind the total synthesis of intricate natural products (like complex alkaloids, terpenes, antibiotics, and the landmark syntheses of molecules such as vitamin B12, palytoxin, and Taxol) as well as the manufacture of pharmaceuticals and fine chemicals. It is also the conceptual basis for computer-aided synthesis planning, where modern software and increasingly machine-learning/AI tools (trained on vast reaction databases) propose retrosynthetic routes, automating and accelerating the discovery of synthetic pathways. Retrosynthesis exemplifies strategic, mechanism-informed thinking in chemistry — combining knowledge of reactions, functional-group chemistry, and stereochemistry into a coherent plan to construct essentially any desired molecule, a capability central to making the medicines, materials, and molecules that modern society depends on.
